_readme.what: Single source of truth for THE TOKENS page (posters-laws.html). The page renders entirely from this file: the Start Here threshold band from `threshold`, and the 12-law grid from `laws`. To add or swap a Token, edit here only.
_readme.concept: The manual to the happy 1% as art. Law Zero is the threshold (Start Here). Laws I-XII run in the manual's order (Seeing Reality = Law One, Usage Protocol = Law Twelve). Each law is a ROOM that can hold several pieces; the one marked featured:true is what shows on the grid. The rest wait in the pool and rotate in.
_readme.how-to-add: Find the law, append a piece to its `pieces[]` with: slug, img, alt, status. Set status:'featured' on the one to show (only one featured per law). Others are 'pool'. To retire a loved piece, set status:'retired' and it moves to the Gallery, off this page.
_readme.status-values: featured = shown on the grid now · pool = queued, next in line when the featured one sells out · retired = pulled from the Tokens page, still buyable in the Gallery.
_readme.edition-cap: `cap` is the limited-edition size (Elena's rotation model: ~50, then retire). These are print-on-demand (Printify), so nothing is pre-bought. The cap is enforced by setting the Shopify product inventory to `cap`; at 0 it auto-shows sold-out and we swap the next pool piece to featured. NEEDS-VERIFY: confirm Shopify inventory-cap behavior on a POD product in Elena's actual store before relying on auto sold-out.
_readme.buyUrl: null until the Shopify Buy Button variant IDs exist. Null = browsable, not yet buyable (coming-soon state). Mirrors prints.json.
_readme.story-rule: Per prints.json the-rule: a print does not ship without a true story (a real Journal entry in `article`). These Tokens are NOT story-backed yet, so `article` is null and buyUrl stays null. Law-room copy + story get written via /caption before anything goes buyable.
_readme.DRAFT-cta: Every `cta` here is a PENNY DRAFT placeholder to show the slot. The real, never-repeating CTAs (a token to live by each law) get written through /caption once the lineup locks. Do not treat these as final copy.
_readme.PROVISIONAL-map: The image->law mapping is Penny's proposed placement (Elena 2026-07-21: 'use what you have for now and we will swap after'). Images swap freely; the law structure is the fixed part.
_readme.law-text: `law` and `manual` are verbatim from the full manual (the-manual-v3, the richer rewrite Elena pasted 2026-07-23). Never reword a law.
_readme.sizes: Every Token sells in the three poster-ladder sizes (see `ladder`). Postcard versions of some pieces live on the separate Postcards surface, not here. Stupid Games (by Rob Goldman) sits outside the 12 in `extras` on its own premium ladder.

ladder._note: Poster ladder. Accessible re-price LOCKED by Elena 2026-07-22, single source of truth = 1-Clients/Eleanora/brand/prints-and-token-ladder.md §5. Never change a price without Elena's word. No free shipping on this line (customer pays Printify's rate). Small 8x10 is 4:5 and center-crops the 2:3 art; the old 20x30 was dropped.

threshold.name: Read This First
threshold.law: The advantage is not knowing; it is executing what everyone knows and almost no one does.
threshold.intro: Every wheel turns on a single point. Cross this one and the other twelve open behind it. Hang it where your mornings begin, dreamer, so the first thing you meet each day is the door.
manual: There are no secrets here. If there were, they would stop working the day they were published. What separates the 1% from everyone else is not hidden information: it is tolerance for the truth, consistency under boredom, and time horizon.

This manual is a map. The territory gets walked. 99% of those who read it will nod, save it somewhere, and do nothing, and that is the first lesson: the door is not locked. It is open, no lock, no line. Almost nobody walks through it because walking through is uncomfortable, and reading feels like progress without the risk of trying.

pieces.alt: A woman in a fur-hooded coat and cream suit reclining on deep blue, rendered in fine ink line

laws.name: Seeing Reality
laws.law: Reality is non-negotiable; your only choice is to see it early or pay for it late.
manual: Your brain did not evolve to see the truth. It evolved to survive and to be liked by the tribe. Which means it ships from the factory with self-deception pre-installed: you see what confirms what you already believe, you defend your mistakes as if they were your children, and you confuse what you want with what is.

The map is not the territory. Your ideas about the world are a model, and every model is wrong somewhere. Whoever treats their opinions as hypotheses improves them; whoever treats them as property defends them all the way to the bottom.

Ask this question of every important belief you hold: what evidence would change my mind? If the answer is “none,” you do not have a belief. You have an identity in disguise, and identities do not update — they break.

Shorten your feedback loops: the faster you find out you were wrong, the cheaper the mistake. Find the smartest person who believes the opposite of what you believe and steal their best arguments. And learn to say “I don’t know” early: ignorance admitted on time is more profitable than a false certainty defended out of pride.
laws.cta: Meet the eyes that stayed open

pieces.alt: A woman haloed in gold before rows of blue Buddha faces, her gaze the only one turned toward you
A woman in a black tee against a wall of blue Buddha faces, gold threading her hair, meeting your eyes

laws.name: Choosing the Game
laws.law: Do not play the wrong game better; choose the game before improving the play.
manual: The 1% of what? That question, which almost nobody asks, decides everything else. Money, status, freedom, mastery, peace of mind: these are different games, with different rules and different prices. Winning one usually costs you points in another, and there are people who win the wrong game with admirable perfection.

Most people never choose. They inherit the game from their parents, their neighborhood, or their feed, and spend their lives competing in a tournament they never signed up for. Stopping to choose is the highest-leverage move there is.

Two hints for choosing well. First: run from zero-sum games. Pure status is zero-sum — for you to rise, someone has to fall — and it poisons whoever plays it. Creation is positive-sum: you can win without anyone losing. Second: prefer games where playing long is an advantage, because that is where patience becomes a weapon.

And the honest math of the 1%: being the top 1% in the world at one single thing requires genetics, luck, and obsession — it is a lottery with very few winning tickets. But being top 10% at two or three things that multiply each other is a decision, not a lottery. The intersection makes you rare, and the competent rare one does not get compared: they get hired, bought, or followed.
laws.cta: A window to the game you chose

pieces.alt: A woman on a New York windowsill in torn-paper collage, smoke and warm light through the glass

laws.name: Agency
laws.law: Act as if you do not need permission, because you do not.
manual: Most barriers are customs dressed up as laws. “It can’t be done” almost always means “it isn’t usually done.” Behind almost every rule holding you back there is a person who invented it on some random Tuesday, with less information than you have right now.

Nobody is coming. There is no committee evaluating whether you deserve to start yet. The permission you are waiting for is not issued by anyone. Ask for the discount, send the message, publish the work, make the call. The real cost of almost every attempt is embarrassment, and embarrassment leaves no scar.

A master’s honesty: some barriers are real. Capital, health, geography, papers, the starting point you were dealt. Denying them is delusion, not optimism. Wisdom lies in telling them apart: imaginary barriers you walk through today; real ones you go around, negotiate with, or erode over years of work. But notice the pattern: the one who treats every barrier as real crosses none.
laws.cta: Hang the proof you stopped waiting

pieces.alt: A woman in a flower crown, black corset and worn jeans, painted in deep reds and marigold over black

laws.name: Bets & Luck
laws.law: Cap the loss, uncap the gain, and repeat until luck finds you.
manual: Stop looking for certainty and learn to bet. Every important decision is a bet with incomplete information; whoever demands certainty before moving has already chosen: they chose not to move.

The formula for good bets is simple and almost nobody follows it: limited downside, open upside. Many cheap attempts beat one heroic bet, because heroism has to be right the first time and volume does not.

Luck exists, it is enormous, and denying its role is the first self-deception of winners — being born where you were born was already a lottery ticket you did not buy. You cannot control luck, but you can control your surface area of exposure to it: luck visits more often the one who makes things and shows them. Make, publish, tell. Opportunity cannot knock on a door it does not know exists.

One unbreakable rule: never bet ruin. Health, freedom, reputation, and the capital that keeps you at the table are never wagered, not for the best payoff in the world. The one who survives every bad hand is sitting at the table when the good one arrives. Survival is the first strategy; all the others are second.

pieces.alt: A woman in magenta tulle suspended in deep blue water, hair drifting
A woman in flowing magenta tulle along a pale shoreline under the words dream bigger this time

laws.name: Compound Interest
laws.law: Plant daily and harvest late — that is the entire secret that never existed.
manual: Compound interest is not a financial concept: it is the law that governs everything that matters. Skills compound, money compounds, reputation, relationships, and health compound. So do debts, grudges, lies, and a sedentary life. Compounding does not judge: it multiplies whatever you feed it.

The cheapest competitive advantage in the world is the time horizon. Almost everyone competes at ninety days; almost nobody competes at ten years. If you are willing to arrive late, you will go further than almost everyone, because by mile twenty there is nobody left running.

Be impatient with actions and patient with results. Most people do the opposite: they wait months to start and demand results in weeks. And understand this down to your bones: boring consistency beats heroic intensity over any period longer than a month. The one who does a little every day buries the one who did a lot once.

pieces.alt: A woman in a rust headscarf cross-legged on a stool amid a collage of pressed sea-grape leaves

laws.name: The Hardware
laws.law: The body is the hardware; no software runs well on a fried machine.
manual: There is no elite mind in a looted body. This is not a health chapter: it is a cognitive performance chapter, because your judgment, memory, creativity, and emotional steadiness run on chemistry, and that chemistry is manufactured by sleeping, moving, and eating.

Sleeping well is the most powerful smart drug in existence and it is free. Every hour you steal from sleep you pay back with interest in worse decisions, shorter memory, and worse moods — and most dangerously: without noticing, because chronic tiredness also damages the judge who was supposed to detect it.

Strength a few times a week, walking every day, morning sun, food your great-grandmother would recognize as food, and no poison in chronic doses. None of this is secret. 99% of people know it and do not do it. There is your first percentile: free, legal, and available tonight at bedtime.
laws.cta: A daily nudge to guard the machine

pieces.alt: A woman lying back with eyes closed, hair loose, in warm terracotta, cream and deep blue

laws.name: Attention
laws.law: Your attention is your only true capital; everything else is a derivative.
manual: You become what you attend to. Your life, in the end, is the sum of the things you paid attention to — nothing else gets in.

There is a trillion-dollar industry designed specifically to fragment your attention and sell it to the highest bidder. In that world, focusing for one straight hour has become a superpower — not because it is intrinsically hard, but because it is scarce: almost nobody can anymore, so whoever can competes alone.

Guard your information diet the way you should guard your food. If alarm, gossip, and cheap opinion go in, anxiety and secondhand thinking come out. Garbage in, garbage thinks. Reserve your best hour of the day for creating, not reacting. Messages, email, and news can wait; your best thought of the day does not come back. And stop killing boredom with the screen: boredom is the womb of ideas, and you are aborting it fifty times a day.
laws.cta: Put it where your eyes land first

pieces.alt: A woman draped in shadow against a red velvet chair and crimson curtains, bathed in deep red

laws.name: The Four Mother Skills
laws.law: Learn, write, sell, multiply; everything else is a branch of these four roots.
manual: There are thousands of skills and four mothers that give birth to them all.

First: learning how to learn. Doing is worth ten times reading. Active recall is worth ten times rereading. Teaching what you learned is the final exam of having understood. Whoever masters this turns any new field into a matter of months, not years.

Second: writing. Writing is thinking with witnesses: the page does not let you cheat the way your head does. Whoever writes clearly thinks clearly, and whoever thinks clearly persuades at scale, without being present, while asleep.

Third: selling. Everything is selling: the idea to the partner, the project to the client, yourself to your own future. Excellence without distribution is invisible, and invisible does not get paid. The world does not reward the best: it rewards the best who also got known.

Fourth: leverage. Pure labor has a linear ceiling: one hour, one result. Code, media, capital, teams, and now machines that think keep working while you are not there. Before every hour invested, ask: does this multiply or evaporate?

pieces.alt: A small figure reaching up against a vast painterly gold-and-orange sunset sky

laws.name: People
laws.law: Be the person people want to play the long game with.
manual: The lone wolf is marketing. Nobody makes it alone; check the biography of anyone you admire and you will find partners, mentors, spouses, friends who lent money, and strangers who opened doors.

Your network is not built by “networking” at events with little business cards. It is built by being useful and keeping your word for years, without keeping score. Reputation is the asset that builds slowest and collapses fastest: twenty years to raise, twenty minutes to fall. Protect it as if it were the business, because it is.

The two biggest decisions of your life — economically and emotionally — are who you partner with and who you build your life with. Give them more analysis than most people give to buying a car, because most people do exactly the opposite.

Cut what is toxic fast: resentment is a tax you pay for someone else’s mistakes. And use envy only as a compass — it tells you with precision what you want — never as fuel, because it is a fuel that burns the tank it sits in.
laws.cta: For the room you actually stand in

pieces.alt: A black-and-white profile, hair pinned and chained, a cigarette and a feather boa, smoke curling into the dark
The same woman in rich oil color, teal and gold baroque behind her, a burgundy feather at her temple

laws.name: The Inner Game
laws.law: Control what is yours, release what is not, and keep the ego in the back seat.
manual: Split the world with a razor: what you control — your actions, your attention, your word — and what you do not: outcomes, other people’s opinions, the past, the weather of the market. All energy spent on the second column is pure loss dressed up as responsible worrying.
Fear almost never signals real danger; it signals the frontier of your growth. Walk toward where it points, with the downside controlled, and you will see the frontier move every time you touch it.

Ego is useful for starting and lethal for navigating. Ego will not let you say “I was wrong,” and whoever cannot say that stops learning, and whoever stops learning has already lost, even if it takes years to find out. Keep your identity light: “I am exploring this” survives the hits; “I AM this” shatters on the first failure.
And equanimity: nothing is as good or as bad as it seems in the first twenty-four hours. Sign nothing, send nothing, quit nothing while hot.
laws.cta: For the wall you face when it goes sideways

pieces.alt: A woman in a cobalt one-piece on the sand, hands on hips, torn planes of orange and blue behind her

laws.name: The Final Trap
laws.law: Win the game without letting the game win you.
manual: Let me save you twenty years: the goal, when you get there, will not feel the way you imagine. The human brain ships from the factory with hedonic adaptation — it moves the goalposts every time you score. This is not a defect you have: it is the design of the species.

And beware this silent law: when the metric becomes the goal, it stops measuring what mattered. You chased money as a measure of freedom and ended up money’s prisoner. You chased an audience as a measure of impact and ended up the algorithm’s slave. The metric was the thermometer, not the fever.

There are people in the top 1% of net worth and the top 1% of misery at the same time. You will meet several and you will not envy them. That is why you must define “enough” in writing before you start, or the game will play you forever.

The greatness that survives an honest audit is this: work that matters done with mastery, people who love you for who you are and not for what you hand out, a mind you can live in peacefully, and a word that is worth something. Get all four complete and you will be in a percentile that money alone does not buy — because almost nobody, at the top or at the bottom, has them all.
laws.cta: Frame your enough before the goalpost moves

pieces.alt: A lone figure on a stormy shoreline beneath a torn sky of navy, cream and gold

laws.name: Usage Protocol
laws.law: The manual does not work; the one who executes it does.
manual: Reading this manual changed you exactly zero. Texts do not change anyone; repetitions do. Here is how it is used:
First: choose one single chapter — the one that made you most uncomfortable to read, because that discomfort is the signal — and execute it for thirty days with one concrete, measurable daily action.
Second: when the cycle closes, write one page. What you did, what happened, what you learned, what comes next. Without that page, the cycle does not count.
Third: next chapter, next cycle. A year holds twelve. Nobody needs all twelve for life to change beyond recognition; four well executed is more than enough.
Fourth: reread the full manual every six months. The text will be the same; you will not. That is why it will tell you things it cannot tell you today.
Fifth: when you master a chapter, teach it to someone. Teaching is the only exam that cannot be cheated.

And the honest master’s final clause: distrust this manual too. Test it against reality, keep what survives contact, and burn what does not. A map that cannot be questioned is not a map: it is a cage. My job was to hand you the map. A good master’s job is to become unnecessary — and in this paragraph, I just did.
laws.cta: For the corner where you do the work

pieces.alt: A woman in a sequined dress reclining in a blue-panelled room with a cooler and stools
